bio.bio_blurb: Connecticut farmer of a Brace family line

narrative_blocks.header: His son outlived the war by weeks
narrative_blocks.body: His boy Orrin lived to see the Confederacy fall — then died that same spring of 1865, barely forty, weeks after the guns went silent. The other children scattered into Barkhamsted harness-making and hill-country farming.

burial.plot_notes: North Cemetery, West Hartford, Hartford County CT (per Findagrave/WikiTree).

military_service.unit: U.S.S. Union and U.S.S. Memphis (US Navy)
military_service.notes: Served as a seaman on the U.S.S. Union and the U.S.S. Memphis during the Civil War. Per NYT obituary 1929: 'naval veteran of the Civil War.'

career.notes: Worked for Adams Express Company for 43 years (per NYT obituary 1929).

sources.short: Findagrave (Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT, Memorial #88979957)
NYT obituary March 16, 1929 — 'Edgar H. Fox, naval veteran of Civil War'

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War naval seaman, USS Union and Memphis
narrative_blocks.body: Edgar Howard Fox served as a Civil War naval seaman aboard the U.S.S. Union and U.S.S. Memphis. After the war he worked for Adams Express Company for forty-three years, lived at 12 Albany Avenue Hartford, and was a member of the Hartford Veteran Volunteer Firemen's Association. He died in his sleep at age 83 in 1929 — his obituary appeared in the New York Times.

burial.plot_notes: North Village Cemetery, New Hartford, Litchfield County CT (per Findagrave).

career.notes: Documented as a harness maker in 1850 census (New Hartford CT) and continued the trade in Barkhamsted CT after his second marriage.

narrative_blocks.body: Charles Brace's second wife Eliza Messenger Bull brought to the marriage a son from her first marriage to Washburn Tuttle Bull: Lester Washburn Bull, born 1847. Lester later took the Brace surname — an adoption-by-name event preserved in WikiTree records. The Brace family thus gained a member through marriage rather than blood, and the Bull name was set aside.

burial.plot_notes: Pine Grove Cemetery, New Hartford, Litchfield County CT (per Findagrave).

parents.father_research_notes: Of Barkhamsted CT (per 1850 census). b.1777 d.1866.

sources.short: Findagrave (Pine Grove Cemetery, New Hartford CT)

burial.plot_notes: Pine Grove Cemetery, New Hartford CT (per Findagrave).

career.notes: Per 1850 US Census: working as tool maker, residing with parents in Barkhamsted CT.

career.organization: Enoch Gaines's piano shop, New Hartford CT
career.notes: Per 1870 US Census: identified as retail piano shop owner. Per 1880 US Census: 'piano maker.' Building and repairing pianos in New Hartford CT.

burial.plot_notes: Pine Meadow Cemetery, New Hartford, Litchfield County CT (per Findagrave).
Pine Meadow Cemetery, New Hartford CT.

sources.short: Civil War draft registration July 1, 1863, Hartford CT

narrative_blocks.header: Edward Hooker recorded his name with question mark
narrative_blocks.body: His given name was uncertainly transcribed in early records as 'Droell(?)' — the question mark indicating that even contemporary clerks could not reliably parse the handwriting. Later genealogical research resolved the name as 'Dwell M. Brace,' the M. possibly Morgan after his maternal grandfather Morgan Goodwin. The original document remains lost.

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War draft registered age forty-three, never called
narrative_blocks.body: Dwell registered for the Civil War draft on July 1, 1863 — age forty-three, residing at 6 Green Street Hartford Connecticut, working as a clerk. He was never called to service in the war. He outlived his first wife Mary Ann Harris and three of their five children before his own death around 1890 in Hartford.
Asenath died in 1824, in her first year.

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford, Hartford County CT (per Findagrave).

narrative_blocks.body: Orelia died in 1832 at the age of one.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Two children married into one Deming sibling pair
narrative_blocks.body: Lory Sr's son Thomas Brace married Electa Alling Deming in 1823, and his daughter Eliza Ann Brace married Walter Deming in 1827. Electa and Walter were brother-and-sister, both children of Theron and Electa Ensign Deming of West Hartford. Two Brace siblings married two Deming siblings within four years — a classic small-town Connecticut close-network pattern.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Henry Brace of West Hartford CT (per Findagrave). EH 396 calls him simply 'Henry Brace'; the 'Captain' title comes from Findagrave/Old Center Cemetery records.
parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Cadwell Brace of West Hartford CT — Cadwell is the same West Hartford family Aaron Cadwell (X00910, husband of Mary Hooker H00986) descends from. The Brace and Cadwell families intermarried with the Hookers across multiple generations.

sources.short: WikiTree — Henry Brace III + Abigail (Hooker) Brace profiles
Findagrave — Pvt Henry Brace III VETERAN (Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT)
Connecticut Rolls and Lists Vol XIII p.234

narrative_blocks.body: He married Abigail Hooker at Hartford in 1763 and they had seventeen documented children between then and approximately 1788. Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy entries 1022-1037 list the children — though crowdsourced records add and subtract from this number in places.

narrative_blocks.header: Revolutionary War private from West Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: He mustered as a private in the Revolutionary War from West Hartford — alongside thousands of other Connecticut farmers and tradesmen who left family homesteads for the Continental ranks. He survived the war and returned to West Hartford where he lived another three decades.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed twelve years before his 1814 death
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Abigail Hooker died at West Hartford on 8 August 1802 after thirty-nine years of marriage. He lived twelve more years alone at West Hartford before his own death on 17 March 1814 at age sixty-nine.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. Buried in row 13 (per Findagrave + WikiTree).

military_service.unit: 4th Company, Connecticut Troops, under Lieut. Charles Seymour
military_service.notes: Enlisted Aug 15, 1776 in the 4th Company, Connecticut Troops under Lieut. Charles Seymour. Marched toward New York Aug 16, 1776 — set sail from New Haven and arrived in New York. Date of discharge not known. Sources: Connecticut Rolls and Lists Vol XIII p.234; West Hartford Church Records; West Hartford Cemetery Records p.41.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Connecticut Deaths and Burials Index, 1650-1934 / Hale Collection

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery (also called North Cemetery), West Hartford CT. Gravestone: "HENRY BRACE & ABIGAIL his wife... She died Aug.07, 1802. EA. 5_." SOURCE: Hale Collection.

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker name preserved as middle name across generations
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Hooker married Henry Brace, and the Hooker surname survived as a middle name through her children, grandchildren, and beyond — a deliberate preservation through collateral lines. Examples include her son Edmond Hooker Brace, her grandson Hezekiah Hooker Brace, and her grandchildren Abigail Hooker Pelton and Edmund Hooker Pelton through her daughter Abigail Hooker Brace Pelton. The pattern preserves Hooker identity across family branches even as primary surnames change.
